6: A direct revelation: the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins. The Christological claim implicit in the healing of the paralytic is staggering: Jesus does not point to God's forgiveness and declare it announced — he forgives in his own name. The scribes' objection (who can forgive sins but God alone?) is the correct theological instinct; Jesus's answer is not to deny the premise but to claim the prerogative: the Son of Man exercises divine authority on earth. The healing that follows is the visible confirmation of the invisible forgiveness, proving the claim.
1: A revelation of God: Jesus crosses back to "his own city" (Capernaum) — the geography positions him as the one who moves between regions at will, without being contained by any single place. The boat-crossing recalls the stilling of the storm (Matthew 8:23-27) and the Gadarene mission; the return structures the healing cycle around his authority. The gathering of a paralytic before him anticipates the eschatological gathering before the throne of the Son of Man (Matthew 25:31-32).
2: A direct revelation: "And behold, some people brought to him a paralytic, lying on a bed. And when Jesus saw their faith, he said to the paralytic, 'Take heart, my son; your sins are forgiven.'" The forgiveness of sins before the healing establishes the priority: the paralysis is the symptom; sin is the root condition. In the OT, forgiveness of sins is a divine prerogative (Isaiah 43:25; Psalms 103:3; Micah 7:18). Jesus speaks forgiveness directly, on his own authority, not as a mediator invoking YHWH's mercy but as the one who forgives. The Christological claim is embedded in the structure of his speech.
3: A direct revelation: "And behold, some of the scribes said to themselves, 'This man is blaspheming.'" The scribes correctly identify the claim Jesus is making — forgiveness of sins is God's prerogative — but draw the wrong conclusion. Blasphemy requires that the claim be false; if Jesus is the Son of God, the forgiveness is genuine and the charge is wrong. Their accusation is the clearest recognition in the passage of what Jesus is claiming, even if they deny the claim. They understand him better than the crowds who simply marvel at the miracle.
4: A direct revelation: "But Jesus, knowing their thoughts, said, 'Why do you think evil in your hearts?'" The knowledge of unspoken thoughts (enthymēseis) is a divine attribute in the OT: YHWH alone searches the heart (Jeremiah 17:10; Psalms 139:1-4; 1 Samuel 16:7). Jesus' access to the scribes' unspoken reasoning is a quiet theophanic detail embedded in the confrontation — his authority to forgive and his authority to know the heart stand together as evidence of his identity.
5: A direct revelation: "For which is easier, to say, 'Your sins are forgiven,' or to say, 'Rise and walk'?" The rhetorical question exposes the logic of the demonstration: words of forgiveness are invisible and unverifiable; the command "rise and walk" has an immediate visible outcome. Jesus will use the visible to authenticate the invisible. The double authority — over sin and over physical paralysis — mirrors the Isaianic vision of the Servant who both heals and forgives (Isaiah 53:4-5).
7: A direct fulfillment: "And he rose and went home." The instantaneous response to Jesus' word — the paralytic rises and walks — fulfills the promise of Isaiah 35:3-6 ("Then shall the lame man leap like a deer") and echoes Psalms 107:20 ("He sent out his word and healed them"). The healed man going to his home is both a practical detail and a theological one: the forgiven, restored man returns to ordinary life as the sign that salvation restores rather than removes from the world.
8: A revelation of God: "When the crowds saw it, they were afraid, and they glorified God, who had given such authority to men." The crowds' response — fear and glorification of God — is the correct theophanic response. Their characterization "given such authority to men" reflects the Danielic Son of Man language (Daniel 7:14: authority given to one like a son of man) without grasping its full Christological weight. The crowds worship God but have not yet identified the one through whom God acts. The Gospel moves toward that identification.
9: A direct revelation: "As Jesus passed on from there, he saw a man called Matthew sitting at the tax collector's booth, and he said to him, 'Follow me.' And he rose and followed him." The call of Matthew (a tax collector — collaborator with Rome, ritually unclean through contact with Gentile coinage) mirrors the calls of Matthew 4:18-22 in structure: "Follow me" → immediate response. The one who commands follows from Peter and Andrew is now commanding a man excluded from normal Jewish religious life. The scope of the call is already beginning to widen beyond the respectable.
10: A revelation of God: "And as Jesus reclined at table in the house, behold, many tax collectors and sinners came and were reclining with Jesus and his disciples." Table fellowship with tax collectors and sinners in the ancient world was not merely social tolerance but a covenant act — sharing food implied acceptance, solidarity, and social identification. Jesus' practice of open table fellowship is the enacted proclamation of the kingdom's inclusive character. It is the shadow of the eschatological banquet (Matthew 8:11; Matthew 22:1-14) available now, ahead of time, to those the religious establishment excluded.
11: A revelation of God: "And when the Pharisees saw this, they said to his disciples, 'Why does your teacher eat with tax collectors and sinners?'" The Pharisees' question is addressed to the disciples, not to Jesus — a social distancing maneuver. Their objection reflects the purity logic that holiness is maintained by separation from the impure. Jesus' response inverts the logic: the holy does not withdraw from the impure but cleanses it by contact. This is the Incarnation principle operating at the level of table fellowship.
12: A direct revelation: "But when he heard it, he said, 'Those who are well have no need of a physician, but those who are sick.'" The physician metaphor identifies Jesus as the one who comes to heal — specifically those who know they are sick, not those who consider themselves healthy. The Christological bearing: the messianic mission is directed toward the lost and the broken (Isaiah 61:1; Luke 4:18-19). The "well" who do not need a physician are those who have not yet recognized their condition — the greater irony in the Sermon's context (Matthew 5:20: "unless your righteousness exceeds that of the scribes and Pharisees").
13: A direct revelation: "Go and learn what this means: 'I desire mercy, and not sacrifice.' For I came not to call the righteous but sinners." The citation of Hosea 6:6 ("I desire steadfast love and not sacrifice") is Jesus' self-authorization for his table fellowship practice. The phrase "I came" (ēlthon) is the language of divine mission — the same construction used in Matthew 5:17 ("I came not to abolish but to fulfill"). The purpose of the coming is the calling of sinners: the mission is not confirmation of the worthy but rescue of the lost.
14: A direct revelation: "Then the disciples of John came to him, saying, 'Why do we and the Pharisees fast, but your disciples do not fast?'" The question about fasting identifies the tension between the penitential practices of the old era and the celebratory character of the new. John's disciples (who had heard John announce the coming kingdom) and the Pharisees (who observed fasting as covenant discipline) both practice mourning-fasting; Jesus' disciples do not. The reason is Christological: the kingdom has arrived in the person of the king.
15: A direct revelation: "And Jesus said to them, 'Can the wedding guests mourn as long as the bridegroom is with them? The days will come when the bridegroom is taken away from them, and then they will fast.'" The bridegroom is a direct messianic self-identification. In the OT, YHWH is the bridegroom of Israel (Isaiah 54:5; Matthew 62:5; Hosea 2:16-20); Jesus appropriates this image for himself. The present time is the wedding feast — fasting would be inappropriate. The "days when the bridegroom is taken away" directly predicts the passion. The verse is one of Matthew's most explicit Christological self-disclosures before the confession at Caesarea Philippi.
16: A revelation of God: "No one puts a piece of unshrunk cloth on an old garment, for the patch tears away from the garment, and a worse tear is made." The new cloth / old garment parable announces that the kingdom Jesus brings is not a repair to existing structures but a new creation. The Christological application: the covenant inaugurated in Christ is the new garment of Isaiah 61:10 ("he has clothed me with the garments of salvation, he has covered me with the robe of righteousness"), not a patch on the Mosaic order.
17: A revelation of God: "Neither is new wine put into old wineskins. If it is, the skins burst and the wine is spilled and the skins are destroyed. But new wine is put into fresh wineskins, and so both are preserved." The new wine of the kingdom requires new containers. The eschatological wine of Isaiah 25:6 ("well-aged wine, wine well refined") and the Messianic banquet cannot be held in the existing institutional structures. This anticipates the church as the new community formed around Christ — a new wineskin appropriate for the wine of the Spirit poured out at Pentecost (Acts 2:13-17).
18: A shadow: "While he was saying these things to them, behold, a ruler came in and knelt before him, saying, 'My daughter has just died, but come and lay your hand on her, and she will live.'" The raising of the dead — here anticipated in the ruler's faith before the act — echoes Elijah's raising of the widow's son (1 Kings 17:17-24) and Elisha's raising of the Shunammite's son (2 Kings 4:32-37). The pattern is: the prophet comes, lays on hands, the child lives. Jesus will fulfill and exceed this pattern.
19: A theme: "And Jesus rose and followed him, with his disciples." The deliberate following of the ruler to his home — Jesus going where the need is — mirrors the pattern of divine condescension in the OT. YHWH descends to hear the cry of his people (Exodus 3:7-8); Jesus rises and follows. The disciples following Jesus as he follows the ruler shows the shape of discipleship: following the one who goes ahead into every place of need and death.
20: A fulfillment: "And behold, a woman who had suffered from a discharge of blood for twelve years came up behind him and touched the fringe of his garment." The woman's twelve-year hemorrhage rendered her ritually unclean (Leviticus 15:25-27), excluded from the covenant community, untouchable. She touches Jesus from behind — in the crowd, without permission. The Christological pattern: the unclean touching the holy should defile the holy; instead the holy cleanses the unclean. This is the incarnation logic, applied in miniature at every healing.
21: A direct revelation: "For she said to herself, 'If I only touch his garment, I will be made well.'" Her faith is specifically in the power resident in Jesus — not in a general hope for divine healing but in the specific conviction that this person, Jesus, is the source of life and healing. The fringe (kraspedon) of his garment may allude to the tassels commanded in Numbers 15:38-40 as reminders of the commandments; if so, the woman reaches for the covenant promise embodied in the one who wears it.
22: A direct revelation: "Jesus turned, and seeing her he said, 'Take heart, daughter; your faith has made you well.' And instantly the woman was made well." The double movement — Jesus perceiving the touch and turning to address her — shows him as fully attentive to the one who reached for him in faith. The commendation "your faith has made you well" grants her an active role; the healing is not merely something done to her but a transaction in which her faith participates. This models the response the Gospel calls for throughout.
23: A shadow: "And when Jesus came to the ruler's house and saw the flute players and the crowd making a commotion." The formal mourning ritual (professional flute players were hired at deaths) is already underway — the death is established as real and the social response as complete. Jesus entering this scene of formal mourning echoes the pattern of Elisha at the Shunammite's house (2 Kings 4:33), where the prophet cleared the room before the raising. The clearing of the mourners is an assertion of authority over the situation.
24: A direct revelation: "He said, 'Go away, for the girl is not dead but sleeping.' And they laughed at him." The dismissal of death as sleep (katheudei) is a Christological claim: what is death from the perspective of human mortality is sleep from the perspective of the one who has power over it. Jesus uses the same word for Lazarus (John 11:11-14) and it appears in Paul's usage (1 Thessalonians 4:13-14). The crowd's laughter is the natural response to what sounds like denial of evident reality; it intensifies the dramatic irony of the raising to follow.
25: A direct fulfillment: "But when the crowd had been put outside, he went in and took her by the hand, and the girl arose." The raising of Jairus's daughter is one of three resurrections before the cross (alongside the widow's son at Nain, Luke 7:11-17, and Lazarus, John 11). The touch and the arising directly echo Elijah (1 Kings 17:21-22) and Elisha (2 Kings 4:34-35) but surpass them: the prophets prayed to YHWH for the restoration; Jesus acts on his own authority, by his own word and touch. The shadow becomes direct revelation.
26: A revelation of God: "And the report of this went through all that district." The spread of the report mirrors the prophetic tradition: YHWH's mighty acts spread through the nations (Joshua 2:10; Psalms 22:27-28). Matthew records the growing fame of Jesus as the accumulation of evidence that the kingdom of God is present in his person and ministry. The healings and raisings are not isolated wonders but signs pointing to the identity of the one who performs them.
27: A direct fulfillment: "And as Jesus passed on from there, two blind men followed him, crying aloud, 'Have mercy on us, Son of David.'" "Son of David" is the primary messianic title in Jewish expectation, rooted in 2 Samuel 7:12-16 and elaborated in Ps 72; Isa 11. The cry of the blind for healing from the Son of David directly fulfills Isaiah 35:5 ("then the eyes of the blind shall be opened"). The blind men perceive — through non-visual faith — what sighted observers miss: the identity of Jesus as the promised Davidic Messiah.
28: A direct revelation: "When he entered the house, the blind men came to him, and Jesus said to them, 'Do you believe that I am able to do this?' They said to him, 'Yes, Lord.'" Jesus requires an explicit faith-confession before the healing — not as a formula but as a genuine question: do they believe in his specific authority? The double use of "Lord" (Kyrie) by the blind men throughout their approach (Matthew 9:27-28) gives their confession its weight: they address the Son of David with the divine title, collapsing the messianic and divine registers.
29: A direct revelation: "Then he touched their eyes, saying, 'According to your faith be it done to you.'" The touch of the eyes recalls the Isaianic vision of the Servant who "opens the eyes that are blind" (Isaiah 42:7). The faith-proportionality principle ("according to your faith") does not make faith the mechanism of healing but positions faith as the appropriate receptive posture toward the one who heals. The healing vindicates the blind men's confession that Jesus is the Son of David able to restore sight.
30: A revelation of God: "And their eyes were opened. And Jesus sternly warned them, 'See that no one knows about it.'" The instruction to silence after a healing — repeated throughout Matthew 8-9 — reflects the "messianic secret" pattern. Jesus is managing the pace and nature of his public disclosure; premature announcement of healing miracles risks reducing him to a wonder-worker rather than allowing the full revelation of his identity through teaching, death, and resurrection. The secret cannot be kept (Matthew 9:31), but the instruction reveals his deliberate control of his own proclamation.
31: A revelation of God: "But they went away and spread his fame through all that district." The inability of the healed to remain silent mirrors the prophetic pattern: when God acts, his acts cannot be contained (Jeremiah 20:9: "If I say, 'I will not mention him, or speak any more in his name,' there is in my heart as it were a burning fire"). The spreading fame is a structural feature of the Matthean narrative: the kingdom's presence cannot be suppressed despite the attempts to manage its disclosure.
32: A fulfillment: "As they were going away, behold, a demon-oppressed man who was mute was brought to him." The mute demoniac immediately follows the blind men — Matthew pairs miracle-types to demonstrate the scope of messianic restoration. The blind see (Matthew 9:30) and the mute will speak (Matthew 9:33), fulfilling Isaiah 35:5-6 ("the eyes of the blind shall be opened... and the tongue of the mute sing for joy"). Matthew's healing narrative in Matthew 8-9 is a deliberate mosaic of Isaianic fulfillment-signs.
33: A direct fulfillment: "And when the demon had been cast out, the mute man spoke. And the crowds marveled, saying, 'Never was anything like this seen in Israel.'" The crowds' verdict — "never was anything like this seen in Israel" — is a direct claim of uniqueness. Unlike the Elijah and Elisha healing narratives, which had OT precedents, the scope and character of Jesus' ministry surpasses anything in Israel's prophetic tradition. The comparison is with all of Israel's history, positioning Jesus not as the next in a line of prophets but as a singular figure.
34: A revelation of God: "But the Pharisees said, 'He casts out demons by the prince of demons.'" The counter-charge — that Jesus' authority comes from Beelzebul rather than from God — is the theological opposition that will be developed in Matthew 12:22-32 (the Beelzebul controversy and the unforgivable sin). The Pharisees correctly identify that the authority is real and extraordinary but attribute it to the wrong source. The irony is that the true source is the Holy Spirit (Matthew 12:28: "it is by the Spirit of God that I cast out demons"), and their attribution to Satan is the specific blasphemy Jesus will warn against.
35: A direct fulfillment: "And Jesus went throughout all the cities and villages, teaching in their synagogues and proclaiming the gospel of the kingdom and healing every disease and every affliction." The summary of Jesus' ministry — teaching, proclaiming, healing — fulfills Isaiah 61:1-2 (the Servant's commission: to bring good news to the poor, healing to the brokenhearted) and Isaiah 35:5-6 (healing signs of the messianic age). Matthew uses this summary formula (cf. Matthew 4:23) to frame the teaching and healing cycles as a unified messianic mission.
36: A revelation of God: "When he saw the crowds, he had compassion for them, because they were harassed and helpless, like sheep without a shepherd." The compassion of Jesus for the scattered sheep fulfills and embodies the divine promise in Ezekiel 34:11-16, where YHWH declares he will himself be the shepherd of the scattered flock, seeking the lost, healing the sick, and binding up the injured. Jesus is the fulfillment of YHWH's shepherding promise — the divine shepherd who has come to his sheep in person.
37: A revelation of God: "Then he said to his disciples, 'The harvest is plentiful, but the laborers are few.'" The harvest metaphor for divine judgment and restoration pervades the prophets (Isaiah 27:12; Joel 3:13; Jeremiah 51:33). Jesus reframes the harvest as primarily a gathering rather than a judgment — the plentiful harvest is the crowds who are ready to receive the kingdom. The "laborers" the harvest Lord sends are the twelve; their commissioning immediately follows. Christ is the Lord of the harvest who sends and also who harvests.
38: A direct revelation: "Therefore pray earnestly to the Lord of the harvest to send out laborers into his harvest." The instruction to pray to the "Lord of the harvest" (kyrion tou therismou) is addressed to the disciples who immediately become the answer to their own prayer (Matthew 10:1-5). The Christological bearing: Jesus both teaches the disciples to pray to the Father for laborers and is himself the one who commissions the laborers in Matthew 10. He is simultaneously the one who sends the prayer and the one through whom it is answered.
